cards.front: What is a keyword (or reserved word) in C++?
cards.back: A keyword is a word reserved by C++ for its own use. C++ has 92 keywords (as of C++23) that have special meaning and cannot be used as identifier names.

cards.front: What is an identifier in C++?
cards.back: An identifier is the name you give to a variable, function, type, or other kind of user-defined item. It's how you refer to that item in your code.

cards.concept: Rules for naming identifiers in C++
cards.explanation:
Identifiers in C++ must follow these rules:
  • Can only contain letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Cannot start with a number
  • Cannot be a C++ keyword
  • Are case-sensitive (myVar and MyVar are different)

cards.question: Which of these identifier names are valid?
cards.answer:
Valid: count, Count, _count, count2
Invalid: 2count (starts with number), my-count (contains hyphen)
cards.explanation: Identifiers can contain letters, numbers, and underscores, but cannot start with a number or contain special characters like hyphens. C++ is case-sensitive, so count and Count are different identifiers.

Follow these conventions for clear, maintainable code:
  • Make names descriptive and clear (count, not c)
  • Start variable names with lowercase letter
  • Use camelCase or snake_case consistently
  • Avoid names starting with underscore (reserved for implementation)
  • Function names typically start with a verb
  • Don't use abbreviations unless very common
examples: int studentCount;      // Good: clear and descriptive
void calculateAverage(); // Good: verb for function

cards.text: Variable names should begin with a {{c1::lowercase}} letter. Function names typically start with a {{c2::verb}} (like calculate, get, set).

cards.front: Why should you avoid identifier names starting with an underscore?
cards.back: Identifiers starting with an underscore are reserved for the C++ implementation and standard library. Using them can cause naming conflicts and undefined behavior.

cards.text: {{c1::snake_case}} uses underscores between words (my_variable), while {{c2::camelCase}} capitalizes each word except the first (myVariable).

cards.front: Is there a practical limit on identifier length in C++?
cards.back: The C++ standard says identifiers can be of any length, but practical limits exist. Most compilers support at least 1024 characters. However, keep names concise and readable.

cards.question: Which variable name is better and why?
cards.answer: Option 2 is better - it's descriptive and self-documenting
cards.explanation: While both are valid, numberOfStudents clearly indicates what the variable represents. Code is read far more often than written, so clarity is more important than brevity.

cards.front: Are myVariable and MyVariable the same identifier in C++?
cards.back: No. C++ is case-sensitive, so myVariable and MyVariable are two completely different identifiers.
